title: Growth and evolution
summary: Growth changes what a business costs to run, how much control its founders keep and how much risk it carries — long before it changes the bottom line. This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 1.5: economies and diseconomies of scale and what they do to average cost; internal (organic) versus external (inorganic) growth; the methods of external growth — mergers and acquisitions, takeovers, joint ventures, strategic alliances and franchising; the reasons for and consequences of growth; why many businesses deliberately choose to stay small; and the role of small businesses in an economy. Every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis and evaluation — so you can see why a supported judgement, not a memorised list, is what earns the top band.
learningObjectives: Explain economies and diseconomies of scale and analyse their effect on a firm's average cost as output changes.
Distinguish internal (organic) growth from external (inorganic) growth and evaluate the trade-offs of each.
Describe the methods of external growth — mergers and acquisitions, takeovers, joint ventures, strategic alliances and franchising — and compare their control, cost, risk and speed.
Explain the reasons for growth and evaluate its consequences for the business and its stakeholders.
Explain why some businesses choose to remain small and analyse the role of small businesses in an economy.
Recommend a growth method for a business by applying the concepts to context and reaching a supported judgement.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Getting bigger — and knowing when not to
simpleExplanation.summary: Growth is not free. As a business gets bigger it can spread its costs over more output and become cheaper per unit — but grow too far, too fast, and it becomes clumsy, slow to communicate and dearer per unit. On top of that, HOW a firm grows — building itself up quietly or buying and teaming up with others — decides how much control, cash and risk it takes on. Some businesses grow as fast as they can; others deliberately stay small because small suits their aim better.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of growth like moving from a bicycle to a bus. On a bike you go where you like the instant you decide — that is a small business with organic growth: full control, low cost, but you can only carry so much. Buy a bus and you can carry far more people and share the fuel cost across all of them, so the cost per passenger falls — those are economies of scale. But a bus needs a driver, a schedule, a depot and a mechanic; if you buy a whole fleet overnight by merging with another bus company, you inherit their drivers, their routes and their culture, and suddenly nobody is sure who is in charge — that is diseconomies of scale and the integration risk of external growth. Bigger moves more people, but only if you can still steer it.
steps: Start with cost. As output rises, economies of scale pull average cost down; push output too far and diseconomies of scale push average cost back up.

Choose a path. Internal (organic) growth builds the business from its own resources — slower, cheaper, safer, full control. External (inorganic) growth joins with or buys other firms — faster, but costlier and riskier.
Pick a method of external growth. Mergers and acquisitions/takeovers, joint ventures, strategic alliances and franchising each trade off control, cost, risk and speed differently.
Weigh the consequences. Growth can cut costs and raise market power, but it can strain finance, culture and quality — which is exactly why some firms choose to stay small.

flashcards.back: The cost advantages a business gains as it increases its scale of output, causing average (per-unit) cost to FALL. Sources include purchasing, technical, financial, marketing and managerial economies.

The cost disadvantages a business suffers when it grows too large, causing average cost to RISE. Usually caused by communication breakdown, poor coordination and falling employee motivation in a big organisation.
Total cost divided by quantity of output (AC = TC / Q). Economies of scale lower it; diseconomies of scale raise it. This is the number to track when judging whether growth helped.
Expansion using the business's OWN resources — opening new outlets, launching new products, entering new markets. Typically slower and cheaper, keeps full control, lower risk.

Expansion by combining with or buying OTHER businesses — mergers, acquisitions/takeovers, joint ventures, strategic alliances. Faster but usually costlier and riskier, with integration and culture-clash problems.
Two (often similar-sized) companies AGREE to combine into a single new company. It is mutually agreed and friendly; both sets of owners consent and a new entity is formed.
One company BUYS a controlling stake in another, which is then absorbed. It can be friendly or hostile (the target's board resists). The buyer ends up in control; no new equal entity is created.
Two or more businesses create a SEPARATE new entity to run a specific project, sharing the capital, risk, control and profit — while each parent stays independent otherwise.

Two or more businesses agree to cooperate for a specific purpose (sharing technology, distribution, etc.) WITHOUT forming a new legal entity. Weaker and more flexible than a joint venture; each firm stays fully independent.

A franchisor sells the right to use its brand, products and business model to a franchisee, who provides the capital and runs the outlet, paying an initial fee and ongoing royalties. It lets the franchisor grow fast with little of its own capital.

The franchisor owns the brand and business format and grants the rights; the franchisee pays for those rights and operates a local outlet. The franchisor gains reach and fees but gives up day-to-day control of quality.
Higher profit, larger market share and market power, economies of scale (lower unit cost), spreading risk through diversification, and managerial motives such as status and salary.

To keep full control and flexibility, serve a niche or personalised market, avoid diseconomies of scale and heavy finance/debt, and match the owner's personal aims. Small is a deliberate choice, not always a failure to grow.
A merger is a MUTUALLY AGREED combination into a new company; a takeover/acquisition is one firm BUYING another (and can be hostile). Do not use the words interchangeably in an exam answer.
BOTH are about AVERAGE (per-unit) cost, not total cost. Total cost normally rises as output grows; the exam point is what happens to cost PER UNIT — economies lower it, diseconomies raise it.

quickCheck.prompt: A factory doubles its output and its TOTAL cost rises, but its cost PER UNIT falls from $8 to $6. This is best described as:
options: Diseconomies of scale, because total cost rose
Economies of scale, because average cost per unit fell
Neither, because total cost changed
A loss, because costs increased
quickCheck.answer: Economies of scale, because average cost per unit fell

quickCheck.prompt: A very large firm finds decisions now take months because messages pass through many layers of managers, and its average cost per unit is rising. This is an example of:
options: Purchasing economies of scale
A financial economy of scale
Diseconomies of scale caused by communication and coordination problems
Internal (organic) growth
quickCheck.answer: Diseconomies of scale caused by communication and coordination problems

quickCheck.prompt: A supermarket chain grows by opening 20 new stores of its own, funded from its retained profit. This is best classified as:
options: External (inorganic) growth, because it opened new stores
Internal (organic) growth, because it used its own resources to expand
A merger, because more stores are involved
A joint venture, because it is a large project
quickCheck.answer: Internal (organic) growth, because it used its own resources to expand

quickCheck.prompt: Two airlines of similar size mutually agree to combine and form a single new company. This is a:
options: Takeover, because one buys the other
Strategic alliance, because they cooperate
Merger, because both agree to join into one new company
Franchise, because it is in the same industry
quickCheck.answer: Merger, because both agree to join into one new company

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement correctly describes franchising from the franchisor's point of view?
options: The franchisor provides all the capital for each new outlet and keeps all the profit
The franchisor grows its brand quickly using the franchisee's capital, but gives up some control over quality and standards
Franchising forms a new separate legal entity jointly owned by both parties
The franchisor buys out the franchisee's business in a hostile takeover
quickCheck.answer: The franchisor grows its brand quickly using the franchisee's capital, but gives up some control over quality and standards

faq.q: What is the difference between a merger and a takeover (acquisition)?
faq.a: A merger is a mutually agreed combination: two companies, often of similar size, consent to join together and form a single new company. A takeover (or acquisition) is one company buying a controlling stake in another, which is then absorbed into the buyer. A takeover can be friendly or hostile — 'hostile' meaning the target company's board does not want to be bought. The quick test: a merger is 'we agree to become one'; a takeover is 'I buy you'.

faq.q: Are economies of scale guaranteed if a business gets bigger?
faq.a: No. Economies of scale are only POTENTIAL cost savings that a business must actively manage to achieve — through bulk buying, better technology, cheaper finance and so on. A firm can grow larger and still fail to capture them if it is badly run, and if it grows too far it can tip into diseconomies of scale, where average cost rises again because the organisation becomes hard to communicate across and coordinate.

faq.q: What is the difference between a joint venture and a strategic alliance?
faq.a: In a joint venture the businesses create a SEPARATE new legal entity to run a specific project and share its capital, risk, control and profit. In a strategic alliance the businesses simply agree to cooperate — sharing technology, research or distribution — WITHOUT forming a new entity; each stays fully independent. A joint venture is the deeper, more formal commitment; a strategic alliance is looser and easier to exit.

faq.q: Why would a business deliberately choose to stay small?
faq.a: Staying small is often a rational choice, not a failure. Owners may want to keep full control and quick decision-making; the market may be a niche where personal service and flexibility matter more than volume; growth would require finance and debt the owners do not want; and staying small avoids the diseconomies of scale, culture clashes and loss of autonomy that expansion brings. Many owners also simply value work-life balance over maximum size.

faq.q: How do I decide which growth method a case-study business should use?
faq.a: Weigh four things against the specific business: CONTROL (organic growth and opening own outlets keep the most; franchising and alliances give some away), COST and finance (external growth and own-outlet expansion need capital; franchising shifts that cost to franchisees), RISK (buying or merging brings integration and culture-clash risk; a niche may not need to grow at all), and SPEED (external growth and franchising expand fastest; organic growth is slow). There is no single best method — the marks come from applying these factors to the given context and justifying a choice.

sections.content: From a single stall to a global chain, growth is one of the most tempting stories in business — but it is also one of the most misjudged. Getting bigger can spread costs across more output and lower the price of each unit, yet grow too far and the same firm becomes slow, hard to coordinate and dearer per unit than before. And the WAY a business grows — quietly building itself up, or buying, merging with and teaming up with others — decides how much control it keeps, how much cash it must find and how much risk it swallows. This lesson covers the whole of syllabus 1.5: economies and diseconomies of scale, internal versus external growth, the methods of external growth, the reasons for and consequences of growth, and why plenty of businesses decide that staying small is the smarter move. Because 1.5 is evaluation-heavy, the worked examples are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why a supported judgement outscores a memorised list every time.

At the centre of growth is 'scale' — the level of output a business produces. As a firm increases its scale, its relationship with cost changes. At first, growing bigger lets it become more efficient and cut its average cost per unit: this is achieving economies of scale. But if it becomes too large and complex to run, it can become LESS efficient, and average cost starts to rise again: this is suffering diseconomies of scale. The variable that matters in an exam answer is AVERAGE cost — the cost of each unit — not total cost, which normally rises as output grows regardless.

items: **Purchasing economies:** buying inputs in bulk earns discounts, lowering the input cost per unit.
**Technical economies:** larger firms can afford more advanced, efficient machinery whose cost is spread over huge output.
**Financial economies:** banks lend to large, established firms more readily and at lower interest rates.
**Marketing economies:** the cost of an advertising campaign is spread over more units, cutting the marketing cost per unit.
**Managerial economies:** large firms can afford specialist managers (finance, HR, marketing), raising efficiency.

**Diseconomies of scale — the other direction:** in a very large organisation communication slows and distorts, coordinating many departments and locations gets harder, and employees can feel remote and demotivated. All three push average cost back up.

sections.question: A bakery produces 1,000 loaves a week at a total cost of $1,500. After expanding it produces 5,000 loaves at a total cost of $6,000, and after expanding again it produces 12,000 loaves at a total cost of $18,000. Calculate the average cost per loaf at each stage and comment on the presence of economies or diseconomies of scale. [6]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — AC at 1,000 loaves.**
AC = TC / Q = $1,500 / 1,000 = **$1.50 per loaf.**

**Step 2 — AC at 5,000 loaves.**
AC = $6,000 / 5,000 = **$1.20 per loaf.**

**Step 3 — interpret the fall from Step 1 to Step 2.**
Average cost fell from $1.50 to $1.20 as output rose. Lower cost per unit as scale increases is **economies of scale** — for a bakery, most likely purchasing economies (bulk-buying flour cheaper per kilo) and technical economies (a larger, more efficient oven whose cost is spread over more loaves).

**Step 4 — AC at 12,000 loaves.**
AC = $18,000 / 12,000 = **$1.50 per loaf.**

**Step 5 — interpret the rise from Step 2 to Step 4.**
Average cost rose back from $1.20 to $1.50 as output grew further. Higher cost per unit as scale increases is **diseconomies of scale** — likely from the harder task of coordinating a bigger workforce, more waste and complex delivery logistics.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 6 marks:** AO2 (application/quantitative) credits each correct average-cost calculation shown with working — $1.50, $1.20 and $1.50. AO3 (analysis) credits the interpretation: correctly naming economies of scale for the fall and diseconomies of scale for the rise, and linking each to a plausible bakery-specific cause. Note the trap the engine watches for: a candidate who says 'costs rose so this is diseconomies' about the FINAL stage is only credited if they refer to cost PER UNIT — total cost rose throughout, so the analysis mark depends on tracking average cost, not total cost.

sections.content: A business can grow in two fundamentally different ways. Internal (or organic) growth means expanding using its OWN resources — opening new branches, launching new products, entering new markets, investing in extra capacity. It is usually slow, steady and relatively safe, funded from retained profit or modest borrowing, and it keeps full control in the founders' hands. External (or inorganic) growth means combining with or buying OTHER businesses — through mergers, acquisitions, joint ventures or alliances. It is far faster and can deliver instant scale, market share and new capabilities, but it costs more and carries real risks: overpaying, taking on debt, and clashes of culture and systems when two organisations are forced together.

items: **Internal (organic) growth:** expand from within — new stores, new products, new markets, more capacity. Slower and cheaper, keeps control, lower risk, but limited by how fast the firm can generate the funds and demand.
**External (inorganic) growth:** combine with or buy other firms. Fast access to new markets, brands, technology and market share — but higher cost, more debt, and integration/culture-clash risk.
**The core trade-off:** internal growth is about BUILDING (slow but fully controlled); external growth is about BUYING or JOINING (fast but harder to control and integrate).
**Finance matters:** organic growth can often be self-funded; external growth usually needs large external finance, which raises gearing and risk.

sections.content: External growth is not one thing — it is a menu of methods that differ sharply in how much control a firm keeps, how much it costs, how much risk it runs and how fast it delivers. The syllabus names five: mergers, acquisitions (takeovers), joint ventures, strategic alliances and franchising. Knowing the precise difference between them — especially merger versus takeover, and joint venture versus strategic alliance — is exactly what 1.5 questions test.

items: **Merger:** two (often similar-sized) firms MUTUALLY AGREE to combine into a single new company. Friendly and consensual; a new entity is formed. Gains scale and shared strengths, but cultures and systems must be integrated.

**Acquisition / takeover:** one firm BUYS a controlling stake in another and absorbs it. Can be friendly or HOSTILE (the target's board resists). Delivers fast control of the target's brand, market and assets — but risks overpaying and integration failure.

**Joint venture:** two or more firms create a SEPARATE new entity for a specific project, sharing capital, risk, control and profit while staying independent otherwise. Spreads risk and pools expertise (e.g. entering a foreign market), but shared control can breed disputes.

**Strategic alliance:** firms cooperate for a specific purpose (sharing technology, research or distribution) WITHOUT forming a new entity; each stays fully independent. Flexible and low-commitment, but weaker and easier to unravel than a joint venture.

**Franchising:** the franchisor sells the right to use its brand and business model to franchisees, who provide the capital and run each outlet in return for an initial fee and ongoing royalties. Lets the franchisor expand fast with little of its own capital — but it gives up day-to-day control of quality and standards.

sections.content: Precision of terms wins marks here. A merger is MUTUALLY AGREED into a new company; a takeover is one firm BUYING another and can be hostile — do not treat the words as interchangeable. A joint venture forms a NEW separate entity; a strategic alliance does NOT. And in franchising, be clear whose perspective you are writing about: for the franchisor it is a low-capital, fast growth method; for the franchisee it is closer to starting a business under a proven brand. Naming the exact method and its defining feature is an AO1 mark you can bank in one sentence.
Reasons for growth and its consequences

Why do businesses grow at all? The pull comes from several directions: higher absolute profit, a larger market share and the market power that lets a firm influence prices, economies of scale that lower unit cost, spreading risk by diversifying into new products or markets, and sometimes managerial motives — status, salary and empire-building — that serve managers more than owners. But growth is double-edged. Its consequences can be positive (lower costs, stronger brand, more bargaining power over suppliers, resilience) or negative (over-stretched finances and higher debt, diseconomies of scale, diluted quality and brand, culture clashes after a merger, and job losses or stakeholder resistance). Whether growth is worth it depends on the specific firm — which is precisely why 'evaluate' and 'recommend' questions dominate this topic.

items: **Reasons for growth:** higher profit; larger market share and market power; economies of scale (lower unit cost); diversification to spread risk; managerial ambition (status, salary).
**Positive consequences:** lower average costs, stronger brand and reputation, greater bargaining power over suppliers, ability to survive downturns.

**Negative consequences:** strained finances and higher gearing/debt, diseconomies of scale, loss of quality control and brand dilution, culture clashes (especially after mergers/takeovers), redundancies and stakeholder conflict.

**Stakeholder lens:** growth affects employees (jobs, redundancy after integration), customers (consistency and choice), suppliers (bigger orders but tougher terms) and the community (jobs vs local impact) — bringing in stakeholders adds AO3 depth.

sections.content: Why some businesses stay small — and the role of small businesses

Not every business wants to grow, and staying small is often a deliberate, rational choice rather than a failure to expand. Owners may want to keep full control and the ability to decide quickly; the market may be a niche where personal service, craftsmanship and flexibility matter more than volume; growth might demand finance and debt the owners are unwilling to take on; and staying small sidesteps the diseconomies of scale, culture clashes and loss of autonomy that expansion brings. Many owners also simply prize independence and work-life balance over maximum size. Small businesses matter to the wider economy too: collectively they create a large share of employment, drive innovation and competition, serve niche and local needs that large firms ignore, and often supply larger firms as specialist partners.

items: **Why stay small:** keep full control and fast decisions; serve a niche needing personal, flexible service; avoid the finance and debt that growth requires; avoid diseconomies of scale and culture clashes; match the owner's personal aims and lifestyle.

**Role of small businesses in the economy:** create a large share of jobs, drive innovation and competition, serve local and niche markets large firms overlook, and act as specialist suppliers to bigger companies.
**Key framing:** small is a strategy, not a shortfall — the 'best' size depends on the firm's aim, market and owners, exactly the kind of judgement the exam rewards.

**Confusing economies with diseconomies of scale** — and forgetting BOTH are about AVERAGE (per-unit) cost. Total cost usually rises as output grows either way; the point is what happens to cost PER UNIT — economies lower it, diseconomies raise it.

**Blurring internal and external growth** — opening your own new stores from retained profit is INTERNAL (organic) growth; buying, merging with or teaming up with another firm is EXTERNAL (inorganic) growth. Getting this wrong misframes the whole answer.

**Using 'merger' and 'takeover' as synonyms** — a merger is a MUTUALLY AGREED combination into a new company; a takeover/acquisition is one firm BUYING another and can be hostile. They are not the same method.

**Confusing a joint venture with a strategic alliance** — a joint venture creates a NEW separate entity with shared ownership; a strategic alliance is cooperation with NO new entity, each firm staying independent.

**Misdescribing franchising** — the FRANCHISOR grows using the franchisee's capital and gives up some quality control; it does not fund or fully control each outlet. Be clear whose perspective you are writing from.

**Assuming bigger is always better** — treating staying small as automatic failure. Many firms rationally stay small to keep control, serve a niche and avoid diseconomies; the exam rewards recognising that.
**Listing advantages and disadvantages without applying them** — a bare list earns AO1 only; the marks climb when each point is tied to the specific business in the case.
**Recommending without a supported judgement** — an 'evaluate' or 'recommend' answer that gives both sides but never commits to a justified conclusion cannot reach the top band.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

Business Management 1.5 is assessed against three objectives: AO1 rewards relevant knowledge and understanding, AO2 rewards applying that knowledge to the specific business in the stimulus, and AO3 rewards analysis and a balanced evaluation. In the analytic/points scheme each distinct valid point earns credit, but the higher 'evaluate' and 'recommend' marks are reserved for answers that combine APPLICATION to context with a BALANCED evaluation that ends in a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT. Watch how the marks below attach to applied, two-sided reasoning — weighing control, cost, risk and speed — and a justified conclusion, never to a generic list.

sections.question: Recommend whether a successful national restaurant chain should expand internationally through franchising or by opening its own outlets. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
The chain faces a classic trade-off between franchising (a method of external growth for the franchisor) and opening its own outlets abroad (internal, organic growth). The right choice turns on how it weighs control, cost, risk and speed in an unfamiliar overseas market.

Franchising's biggest advantage is speed and low capital cost. Because each franchisee provides the capital to fit out and run their own outlet and pays an initial fee plus ongoing royalties, the chain can enter several countries quickly without funding every kitchen itself. Local franchisees also bring knowledge of local tastes, regulations and property markets, which lowers the risk of misjudging a foreign market. For a chain that is already successful nationally but does not know these overseas markets, that local expertise and shared cost are powerful arguments.

However, franchising means giving up day-to-day control of quality and standards — and for a restaurant chain, consistency of food and service IS the brand. A single franchisee cutting corners on hygiene or ingredients can damage the reputation of every outlet worldwide. The chain also earns only a fee and royalty on each franchised outlet rather than the full profit, so if a market proves highly profitable, franchising leaves most of that upside with the franchisees.

Opening its own outlets reverses these trade-offs. The chain keeps complete control over menu, quality, staff training and brand image, and keeps ALL the profit from each successful restaurant. But this is slow and capital-hungry: it must fund every site itself, hire and train staff from scratch, and carry the full risk of any outlet that fails in a market it does not yet understand. Rapid international expansion this way could stretch its finances and management thin, and may expose it to diseconomies of scale as it tries to coordinate restaurants across many countries.

**Judgement.** On balance, for FAST international expansion into unfamiliar markets, the chain should expand primarily through franchising, but protect its brand by franchising selectively — choosing experienced franchisees, enforcing strict brand standards and quality audits, and perhaps opening a few of its OWN flagship outlets in key cities to anchor the brand. The decisive factor is that international expansion multiplies both the capital needed and the market risk, and franchising transfers much of both to franchisees who understand local conditions; the main drawback — loss of quality control — is real but manageable through tight franchise agreements, whereas the capital and risk of self-funding outlets across many countries is far harder to reverse. Franchising is therefore the appropriate primary method, with owned flagships used to safeguard the brand.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks:**

- **AO1 — knowledge (relevant concepts):** the engine credits accurate command of the ideas the question turns on — franchising as a franchisor's low-capital external growth method (fee plus royalties, franchisee-provided capital), organic growth through own outlets, and the control/quality implications of each. Correct terminology signals AO1.
- **AO2 — application to THIS business:** marks are awarded for tying those concepts to a RESTAURANT chain expanding INTERNATIONALLY — consistency of food and service as the brand, franchisees' knowledge of local tastes and regulations, the capital and management strain of self-funding kitchens abroad. Generic points that never mention the restaurant or the international context do not earn AO2.
- **AO3 — analysis and balanced evaluation:** the engine credits developed two-sided reasoning that weighs the SAME criteria on both sides — control, cost, risk and speed — for franchising versus owned outlets, and, crucially, a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT that commits to a decision and justifies WHY one method outweighs the other in this context.
- **What lifts an answer into the top band:** application plus judgement. The engine rewards an answer that applies the concepts to the restaurant chain and ends in a reasoned recommendation (here, franchising with brand safeguards); it does NOT reward a longer generic list of franchising pros and cons that never decides.
- **What the engine rejects:** all-franchising or all-owned answers with no balance; a 'judgement' that merely restates both sides without choosing; and correct definitions with no link to the restaurant chain's international expansion.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — AO1 for the right concepts (franchising, organic growth, economies of scale, merger vs takeover), AO2 for applying them to the business in the question, and AO3 for balanced analysis ending in a supported judgement. So do not just list advantages and disadvantages: name the concept, apply it to the specific business, weigh both methods against the SAME criteria — control, cost, risk and speed — and finish by committing to a recommendation with a reason. The application and the judgement are where the top-band marks are won.

The ideas in this topic run through the rest of the course. Economies and diseconomies of scale return in operations management and cost analysis; internal versus external growth and the finance it demands feed directly into sources of finance, and into the Ltd-to-plc conversions and mergers you met in types of business entities; and the reasons-and-consequences framework here becomes the backbone of strategic and stakeholder analysis later. Master the habit built in this lesson — identify the concept, apply it to the specific business, weigh both sides against clear criteria, then commit to a justified judgement — and you have the template that earns marks across every evaluation question in Business Management.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: recommend a growth method for a business, weighing control, cost, risk and speed and reaching a supported judgement
